# Nightjar — LLM Reference Document
# Format: llms.txt (structured plaintext optimized for LLM consumption)
# Version: 0.1.0 | Updated: 2026-03-28
# Source: https://github.com/nightjar-dev/nightjar

---

## Overview

Nightjar is a contract-anchored verification layer for AI-generated code. Developers write
behavioral specifications in `.card.md` files — YAML frontmatter plus Markdown body. An LLM
generates the implementation. Nightjar runs a six-stage pipeline (preflight, dependency audit,
schema validation, property-based testing, negation-proof validation, and Dafny formal
verification) and either produces a mathematically verified artifact or a concrete counterexample
via a CEGIS (Counterexample-Guided Inductive Synthesis) retry loop. Code is regenerated from
scratch on every build and is never manually edited. The central architectural principle:
generated code is disposable; specs are the source of truth.

---

## Install

```bash
pip install nightjarzzz
```

PyPI package name: nightjarzzz
CLI entry point: nightjar
Python: 3.11+

### Install with development extras

```bash
pip install "nightjarzzz[dev]"
```

### Install from source

```bash
git clone https://github.com/nightjar-dev/nightjar
cd nightjar
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

### External dependencies (not on PyPI)

- Dafny 4.x — download binary from https://github.com/dafny-lang/dafny/releases
  - Required for Stage 4 formal verification (complex functions)
  - Optional if using CrossHair-only mode (--fast flag)
- Set NIGHTJAR_MODEL environment variable to select the LLM model

---

## Core Concepts

### .card.md Specs

The `.card.md` file is the single source of truth. It has two parts:

1. YAML frontmatter: machine-readable contract (module boundary, inputs/outputs, invariants)
2. Markdown body: human-readable intent, acceptance criteria, functional requirements

Specs live in `.card/` at the project root. Generated code lives in `.card/audit/` (read-only).
Never edit generated code directly — all changes go through the spec.

### Trust Levels (Invariant Tiers)

Every invariant in a `.card.md` has a tier that determines how it is verified:

- `example`: generates a unit test from Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (pytest)
- `property`: generates a Hypothesis property-based test with 200+ random inputs
- `formal`: generates Dafny preconditions/postconditions requiring mathematical proof

### The Five-Stage Verification Pipeline

Nightjar runs stages in order, cheapest first, short-circuiting on failure:

```
Stage 0  Pre-flight         ~0.5s   Python AST well-formedness + YAML self-validation
Stage 1  Dependency check   ~1-2s   Sealed manifest diff + pip-audit CVE scan
Stage 2  Schema validation  ~0.5s   Pydantic v2 parse of generated output schemas  [parallel]
Stage 3  PBT               ~3-8s   Hypothesis property-based tests from invariants [parallel]
Stage 2.5 Negation-proof   ~2-5s   CrossHair checks negated formal invariants (degenerate spec guard)
Stage 4  Formal            ~5-20s  CrossHair (complexity <= 5) or Dafny (complexity > 5)
```

Stages 2 and 3 run in parallel (asyncio.gather). Stage 2.5 is skipped when no `formal` invariants
are present. Stage 4 routes between CrossHair and Dafny based on cyclomatic complexity score.

### CEGIS Retry Loop

When any stage fails, Nightjar enters a CEGIS repair loop:

1. Collect failure context (counterexample, Dafny error lines, assertion batch ID)
2. Build a structured repair prompt (spec + failed code + error block + prior attempts)
3. Call LLM via litellm at temperature 0.2
4. Re-run full pipeline from Stage 0
5. Retry up to N=5 times (configurable with --retries)
6. On exhaustion: LP dual root-cause diagnosis identifies the binding constraint

Success rate: 86% pass@10 with Claude 3.5 Sonnet (DafnyPro benchmark).

### Code Generation Pipeline

Before verification, code is generated via a three-agent pipeline:

```
Spec Rewriter   →  applies 19 proven rewrite rules to normalize ambiguous invariants
Analyst Agent   →  reads intent + acceptance criteria, outputs structured requirements
Formalizer      →  reads analyst output + invariants, outputs Dafny module skeleton
Coder           →  reads Dafny skeleton, outputs complete implementation
```

All three agents call the LLM via litellm. Model is configurable via NIGHTJAR_MODEL env var.

### Immune System

The immune system monitors production and feeds discovered invariants back into specs:

1. Signal collection: OpenTelemetry spans + MonkeyType type traces + Sentry errors
2. Invariant candidate generation: reimplemented Daikon algorithm + LLM enrichment
3. Invariant verification: CrossHair symbolic + Hypothesis PBT
4. Quality scoring: Wonda 4-criteria filter (precision, recall, specificity, stability >= 0.8)
5. Adversarial debate: Proposer LLM vs Adversary LLM with judge scoring
6. Spec integration: verified invariants appended to `.card.md`, next build is safer

---

## CLI Reference

```
nightjar <command> [flags]
```

### Commands

```
nightjar init [module-name]
    Scaffold a new .card.md spec + deps.lock + test stubs
    Example: nightjar init payment

nightjar generate [--model MODEL]
    LLM generates code from all .card.md specs
    Uses Analyst → Formalizer → Coder pipeline
    Model defaults to NIGHTJAR_MODEL env var
    Example: nightjar generate --model claude-sonnet-4-6

nightjar verify
    Run full 6-stage verification pipeline (Stages 0–4 + 2.5)
    Exit 0 = all pass, Exit 1 = verification fail

nightjar verify --fast
    Run Stages 0–3 only (skip Stage 2.5 and Dafny)
    Suitable for rapid iteration; no formal proof

nightjar verify --stage N
    Run only stage N (0, 1, 2, 3, 4)

nightjar verify --tui
    Run verification with Textual TUI dashboard (live stage status)

nightjar build [--target LANG]
    Full pipeline: generate + verify + compile to target language
    Targets: py | js | ts | go | java | cs
    Example: nightjar build --target py

nightjar ship
    build + cryptographically sign the verified artifact

nightjar retry [--max N]
    Force re-entry into the CEGIS retry loop
    Default max retries: 5

nightjar lock
    Freeze all current dependencies into deps.lock with SHA-256 hashes
    Run after adding any new dependency

nightjar explain
    Display the last verification failure with LP dual root-cause diagnosis
    Identifies which invariant constraint is structurally infeasible

nightjar optimize
    Run DSPy SIMBA prompt optimization on the generation pipeline
    Optimizes Analyst/Formalizer/Coder prompts for higher pass rates
```

### Flags (all commands)

```
--contract PATH     Path to .card.md (default: ./.card/*.card.md)
--target LANG       Compile target: py | js | ts | go | java | cs
--model NAME        LLM model (default: NIGHTJAR_MODEL env var)
--retries N         Max CEGIS repair attempts (default: 5)
--output DIR        Output directory for compiled artifacts
--tui               Launch Textual TUI dashboard
--ci                CI mode: strict, no prompts, exit code on failure
--fast              Skip Stage 2.5 + Dafny (fast check only)
--stage N           Run only stage N
```

### Exit Codes

```
0   All verification stages PASS
1   Verification FAIL (at least one stage failed)
2   Configuration error (bad .card.md, missing nightjar.toml)
3   Timeout exceeded
4   LLM API error
5   Max retries exceeded — human escalation required
```

### Environment Variables

```
NIGHTJAR_MODEL      LLM model name (e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6, openai/o3, deepseek/deepseek-chat)
```

---

## Pipeline Stages — Detailed

### Stage 0: Pre-flight (preflight.py)

- Parses all Python source with the `ast` module — catches syntax errors before anything else
- Validates `.card.md` YAML schema (required fields: card-version, id, title, status, module,
  contract, invariants)
- Fast: ~0.5 seconds, $0.00 cost
- Short-circuits on malformed input

### Stage 1: Dependency Check (deps.py)

- Compares imports in generated code against the `deps.lock` allowlist
- Runs `uv pip sync --require-hashes --dry-run` to verify sealed manifest integrity
- Runs `pip-audit --requirement deps.lock --format json` for CVE scanning
- Motivation: 19.7% of AI-generated package dependencies are hallucinated (slopsquatting)
- Short-circuits on unknown packages or known CVEs
- Run `nightjar lock` to refresh deps.lock after adding a new dependency

### Stage 2: Schema Validation (schema.py)

- Parses generated output data structures against the `contract.outputs` schema using Pydantic v2
- Validates OpenAPI contract definitions when present
- Runs in parallel with Stage 3
- Short-circuits on schema mismatch

### Stage 3: Property-Based Testing (pbt.py)

- Generates Hypothesis test code from `invariants` entries with `tier: property`
- Runs with `max_examples=200` and `derandomize=True` for reproducibility
- On failure: extracts minimal counterexample (input, output, property text)
- Counterexample is fed into the CEGIS repair prompt
- Runs in parallel with Stage 2

### Stage 2.5: Negation-Proof Validation (negation_proof.py)

- Only runs when `formal` invariants are present
- Negates the postconditions of each formal invariant
- Runs CrossHair on the negated spec — if CrossHair finds the negation trivially true, the
  invariant is degenerate (vacuously satisfied)
- Guards against specs that pass Dafny not because the code is correct but because the invariant
  is so weak that any code satisfies it
- Short-circuits with "weak spec" error if degenerate invariant detected

### Stage 4: Formal Verification (formal.py)

- Routes based on complexity score: cyclomatic_complexity + floor(ast_depth / 3)
- Score <= 5: CrossHair symbolic execution (~13s avg, ~70% wall-time saving)
- Score > 5: Dafny 4.x formal verification
  - Flags: --verification-time-limit 15 --isolate-assertions --boogie /vcsCores:4
- Only verifies invariants with `tier: formal`
- Graceful degradation: Dafny timeout → CrossHair → Hypothesis extended → confidence-scored partial
- Short-circuits on proof failure; structured error fed into CEGIS repair prompt

---

## .card.md Format

Every module has one `.card.md` file in `.card/`. Minimum viable spec is ~30 lines.

### Full Format

```yaml
---
card-version: "1.0"
id: module-name
title: Human-Readable Module Title
status: draft | review | approved | frozen

module:
  owns: [function_a(), function_b(), EntityX]
  depends-on:
    - other-module: "approved"
    - external-service: "^3.x"
  excludes:
    - "feature explicitly out of scope"

contract:
  inputs:
    - name: param_name
      type: integer
      constraints: "param_name > 0 AND param_name <= 1_000_000"
  outputs:
    - name: ResultType
      type: object
      schema:
        field_a: string
        field_b: integer
  errors:
    - SpecificError
  events-emitted:
    - event.name

invariants:
  - id: INV-001
    tier: property
    statement: "A valid token always corresponds to exactly one active user session"
    rationale: "Prevents session hijacking"

  - id: INV-002
    tier: formal
    statement: "Output amount is always strictly less than or equal to input amount"
    rationale: "Financial safety — cannot create money"

constraints:
  performance: "p95 latency < 2000ms"
  security: "all tokens expire within 24 hours"
  idempotency: "calling login() twice with same credentials returns same token"
---

## Intent

Natural language description of what this module does and why it exists.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Story 1 — Login (P1)

**As a** user, **I want** to log in with email/password, **so that** I get a session token.

1. **Given** valid credentials, **When** login() is called, **Then** a JWT is returned
2. **Given** invalid password, **When** login() is called, **Then** AuthError is raised

### Edge Cases

- What happens when the account is locked? → LockError raised
- [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: What about OAuth tokens?]

## Functional Requirements

- **FR-001**: System MUST validate email format before checking database
- **FR-002**: System SHOULD log all authentication attempts
- **FR-003**: System MAY cache valid tokens for 5 minutes
```

### Minimum Viable .card.md (~30 lines)

```yaml
---
card-version: "1.0"
id: user-auth
title: User Authentication
status: draft
module:
  owns: [login(), logout(), validate_token()]
  depends-on: [postgres, bcrypt]
contract:
  inputs:
    - name: email
      type: string
    - name: password
      type: string
  outputs:
    - name: session_token
      type: string
invariants:
  - id: INV-001
    tier: property
    statement: "A valid token always corresponds to exactly one active user session"
---

## Intent
Let users log in with email/password and get a session token.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Story 1 — Login (P1)
1. **Given** valid credentials, **When** login() is called, **Then** a JWT is returned
2. **Given** invalid password, **When** login() is called, **Then** AuthError is raised
```

### Invariant Tier Summary

```
tier: example   → unit test from Given/When/Then (pytest)
tier: property  → Hypothesis PBT, 200+ random inputs (fast, ~3-8s)
tier: formal    → Dafny mathematical proof or CrossHair symbolic (~5-20s)
```

Escalate tiers for higher-risk invariants. Security and financial invariants should use `formal`.

---

## Architecture

### Source Layout

```
src/
├── nightjar/              Main CLI and verification pipeline
│   ├── cli.py             Click commands — 8 commands
│   ├── parser.py          .card.md parser (YAML + Markdown)
│   ├── generator.py       Analyst → Formalizer → Coder pipeline
│   ├── verifier.py        Pipeline orchestrator (asyncio)
│   ├── spec_rewriter.py   19 proven spec rewrite rules (pre-generation)
│   ├── retry.py           CEGIS retry loop
│   ├── diagnosis.py       LP dual root-cause diagnosis
│   ├── negation_proof.py  Stage 2.5 negation-proof validation
│   ├── oracle_lifter.py   Test oracle lifting (tests → .card.md invariants)
│   ├── tui.py             Textual TUI dashboard
│   ├── display.py         Rich streaming DisplayCallback protocol
│   ├── sentry_integration.py  Sentry errors → immune system feed
│   ├── gitnexus_hooks.py  Blast radius warnings before regeneration
│   ├── mcp_server.py      MCP server (3 tools)
│   ├── invariant_generators/  Per-tier code generators
│   ├── security/          OWASP security checks
│   └── stages/
│       ├── preflight.py   Stage 0
│       ├── deps.py        Stage 1 (uv + pip-audit)
│       ├── schema.py      Stage 2 (Pydantic v2)
│       ├── pbt.py         Stage 3 (Hypothesis)
│       └── formal.py      Stage 4 (CrossHair + Dafny)
└── immune/                Immune system
    ├── collector.py       Trace collection (MonkeyType + OpenTelemetry)
    ├── daikon.py          Daikon algorithm reimplementation (MIT license)
    ├── mines.py           Invariant mining pipeline
    ├── enricher.py        LLM enrichment of invariant candidates
    ├── enforcer.py        Runtime enforcement + temporal supersession
    ├── quality_scorer.py  Wonda quality scoring (4-criteria filter)
    ├── debate.py          Adversarial debate validation
    └── spec_updater.py    Append invariants to .card.md
```

### Tech Stack

```
Language             Python 3.11+
CLI framework        Click
LLM interface        litellm (model-agnostic, 100+ providers)
Verification engine  Dafny 4.x (compiles to py/js/go/java/cs)
Property-based test  Hypothesis
Schema validation    Pydantic v2
Symbolic execution   CrossHair (Z3-based)
Runtime contracts    icontract
Type tracing         MonkeyType
Dependency security  uv + pip-audit
TUI dashboard        Textual
Streaming output     Rich
MCP server           mcp SDK
```

### MCP Server

Nightjar ships as an MCP server for IDE integration (Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code, Kiro).

Three tools:

```
verify_contract(spec_path, code_path, stages?)
    Run Nightjar pipeline on generated code against a .card.md spec
    Returns: { verified, stages, errors, duration_ms }

get_violations(spec_path)
    Get detailed violation report from last verification run
    Returns: { violations: [{ stage, file, line, message, counterexample }] }

suggest_fix(spec_path, violation_id)
    Get LLM-suggested fix for a specific verification violation
    Returns: { suggested_code, explanation, confidence }
```

### DisplayCallback Protocol

```python
class DisplayCallback(Protocol):
    def on_stage_start(self, stage: int, name: str) -> None: ...
    def on_stage_complete(self, result: StageResult) -> None: ...
    def on_pipeline_complete(self, result: VerifyResult) -> None: ...
```

Implementations: RichStreamingDisplay (terminal output), NightjarTUI (Textual), NullDisplay (tests).

---

## Key Files

```
nightjar.toml               CLI configuration (project name, default model, stage flags)
pyproject.toml              Package metadata, entry point: nightjar.cli:main
deps.lock                   Sealed dependency manifest with SHA-256 hashes
.card/*.card.md             Module specs (source of truth — edit these, not generated code)
.card/audit/                Read-only generated code (git-tracked, never manually edited)
.card/verify.json           Last verification run report
.card/cache/                Verification cache (content-hash → verified result)
src/nightjar/cli.py         All CLI commands
src/nightjar/verifier.py    Pipeline orchestrator
src/nightjar/generator.py   Three-agent code generation
src/nightjar/retry.py       CEGIS repair loop
src/nightjar/stages/        Individual stage implementations
src/immune/                 Immune system (trace → invariant → spec update)
docs/REFERENCES.md          Full citation library (read before implementing anything)
docs/ARCHITECTURE.md        Full system design with citations
```

---

## Development

### TDD Workflow

1. Write a failing test FIRST (in tests/unit/ or tests/integration/)
2. Run it to confirm it fails
3. Write minimal code to make it pass
4. Run test to confirm it passes
5. Commit

### Running Tests

```bash
# All tests
pytest tests/ -v

# Unit tests only (no Dafny or LLM required)
pytest tests/ -v -m "not integration"

# Specific test file
pytest tests/unit/test_parser.py -v
```

### Reference-First Development

Every algorithm in Nightjar traces to a citation in docs/REFERENCES.md. Before implementing
any component:

1. Open docs/REFERENCES.md and find the [REF-XXX] entries for the task
2. Read the cited paper or tool documentation
3. Implement from the paper, not from intuition
4. Tag the commit with the reference:
   `ref: implement CEGIS repair loop [REF-NEW-02, REF-C02]`

Do NOT implement from training data. If you cannot find a reference for what you are implementing,
stop and ask.

### Commit Conventions

```
feat: description          new feature
fix: description           bug fix
ref: description [REF-XXX] reference implementation from paper
test: description          test only
docs: description          documentation
```

### Anti-Patterns (What NOT to Do)

- DO NOT edit files in .card/audit/ — they are auto-generated and read-only
- DO NOT call LLM providers directly — all LLM calls go through litellm
- DO NOT hardcode model names — use NIGHTJAR_MODEL env var
- DO NOT add dependencies without running nightjar lock to update deps.lock
- DO NOT use Fuzzingbook code in production — CC-BY-NC-SA prohibits commercial use;
  Nightjar reimplements the Daikon algorithm from scratch in src/immune/daikon.py (MIT)
- DO NOT skip the verification pipeline for "simple" changes

### Configuration (nightjar.toml)

```toml
[nightjar]
model = "claude-sonnet-4-6"   # overridden by NIGHTJAR_MODEL env var
retries = 5
target = "py"

[verify]
fast = false                  # true skips Stage 2.5 + Dafny
```

---

## Scan Results

Nightjar's Stage 1 dependency check (pip-audit) scanned the dependency graph at build time.

Summary: 48 bugs found across 20 packages in the broader Python ecosystem.

All critical CVEs are blocked at Stage 1 before any generated code can use vulnerable packages.
The sealed manifest (deps.lock) with SHA-256 hashes prevents hallucinated or substituted packages
from entering the dependency graph.

For the full audit report: run `nightjar verify` and inspect `.card/verify.json`.

Reference: 19.7% of AI-generated package dependencies are hallucinated (slopsquatting research,
USENIX). The sealed manifest is the primary defense. See docs/REFERENCES.md [REF-P27].

Audit tooling:
- pip-audit: https://github.com/pypa/pip-audit (Apache 2.0)
- uv: https://github.com/astral-sh/uv (MIT)

---
